case_name: Groundwater for potable reuse with RDX, lithium, and strontium under seasonal variability and energy-cost constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for groundwater intended for potable reuse that must control RDX while also reducing lithium and strontium under seasonal water-quality variation, cost limits, and energy sensitivity.

input_data.scenario_background: A water utility is evaluating a contaminated groundwater source for indirect potable reuse blending after advanced treatment. RDX is the primary driver because the utility wants a strong margin of safety for an energetic compound, while lithium and elevated strontium create additional pressure from source-control concerns and finished-water acceptability targets. Seasonal pumping patterns change TDS and nitrate levels, so the preferred option must remain robust during winter high-salinity periods without creating excessive energy demand. More than one route is technically feasible, but the ranking depends on how well each option balances contaminant coverage, energy use, concentrate or spent-media handling, and operational complexity.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water for potable reuse blending should provide reliable RDX control to very low levels and achieve meaningful reduction of lithium and strontium with stable performance across seasonal source-water shifts.
secondary_goals: avoid a treatment train that is overly sensitive to winter salinity increases
control residuals and waste-stream handling burden
maintain moderate operator workload for a municipal utility

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer to minimize high-volume brine disposal but manageable spent media replacement is acceptable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: process should tolerate seasonal TDS and nitrate shifts without large performance swings or excessive retuning

core_unit_functions.cartridge_prefiltration: protect downstream adsorption, ion exchange, and membrane units from particulates
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon_or_GAC_for_R: remove RDX through adsorption and biologically assisted polishing potential
core_unit_functions.weak_acid_cation_exchange_softening: reduce divalent hardness and part of the strontium load while lowering scaling risk for downstream membranes
core_unit_functions.low_pressure_reverse_osmosis_partial_spl: provide lithium reduction and additional strontium and nitrate control on a treated sidestream before blending
core_unit_functions.UV_advanced_disinfection: provide final pathogen control compatible with potable reuse treatment barriers

applicability_rationale: RDX is the primary compliance driver and is well addressed by carbon-based adsorption before membrane treatment
a partial-split low-pressure RO step reduces lithium without forcing full-flow high-energy desalting of the entire groundwater supply
upstream cation exchange softening helps address strontium and reduces membrane scaling and cleaning frequency
this hybrid route balances broad contaminant coverage with lower energy than full-flow RO and lower concentrate volume than an all-membrane design
the process is relatively robust to seasonal TDS variation because the membrane duty is moderated and the carbon step is not strongly driven by salinity
potential_risks: RDX carbon bed life must be verified with pilot or EBCT testing because competing organic matter can shorten run length
lithium rejection in low-pressure RO can vary with membrane selection and recovery
ion exchange regeneration brine and RO concentrate both require disposal planning
seasonal salinity spikes can still increase membrane pressure and reduce recovery if scaling control is not maintained

core_unit_functions.cartridge_prefiltration: protect carbon and ion exchange units
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_dual_bed: provide primary RDX adsorption with staged breakthrough protection
core_unit_functions.strong_acid_cation_exchange: remove hardness and reduce strontium through cation exchange
core_unit_functions.UV_advanced_disinfection: provide final disinfection barrier for potable reuse application

applicability_rationale: this route is lower energy than membrane-led alternatives and avoids RO concentrate management
dual-bed GAC provides a practical and established path for RDX control with improved breakthrough management
cation exchange is effective for strontium reduction because strontium behaves as a divalent cation similar to hardness ions
the train is attractive when the utility places stronger weight on energy and brine volume than on aggressive lithium reduction
potential_risks: lithium removal is limited because conventional cation softening is not a strong solution for monovalent lithium at these concentrations
spent carbon changeout frequency may increase if trace organics or DOC rises
regeneration waste from strong acid cation exchange still needs management even though it is less difficult than RO concentrate in many settings

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_and_antiscalant: control fouling and scaling and stabilize RO operation under variable salinity
core_unit_functions.two_pass_reverse_osmosis: provide strong rejection of lithium, strontium, nitrate, and a broad dissolved contaminant barrier
core_unit_functions.stabilization: remineralize and adjust corrosivity of low-alkalinity permeate

applicability_rationale: full RO offers the broadest dissolved contaminant control and can reduce lithium more reliably than adsorption and conventional ion exchange routes
RO also provides significant strontium reduction and helps with co-contaminants such as nitrate and TDS during winter peaks
this option may be favored if finished-water quality objectives are tightened beyond the stated baseline or if future regulatory drivers expand
potential_risks: high specific energy demand conflicts with the stated energy sensitivity
concentrate disposal is a major challenge for inland groundwater facilities
seasonal TDS increases can raise operating pressure and reduce recovery
RDX rejection should not be assumed as the sole barrier without confirmation because the project still benefits from an upstream dedicated organics control step

core_unit_functions.advanced_reduction_or_UV_based_destructi: chemically transform RDX rather than relying only on adsorption
core_unit_functions.selective_lithium_ion_exchange: target lithium reduction using specialty media
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: provide polishing for divalent ions including strontium and reduce hardness

applicability_rationale: the route is technically capable of addressing all target contaminants with tailored unit operations
specialized lithium media can be attractive where lithium reduction is a strategic objective and brine volumes must be lower than with full RO
nanofiltration can further reduce strontium because divalent ions are generally well rejected
potential_risks: specialized lithium-selective media are less conventional for municipal potable reuse and may have procurement and replacement uncertainty
advanced destruction of RDX is more operationally complex than adsorption-based control
nanofiltration still generates concentrate and may show variable lithium rejection compared with RO
integrating several specialized steps increases operator burden and commissioning risk

core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process: oxidative treatment step for trace organic contaminant reduction
core_unit_functions.conventional_softening: reduce hardness and some strontium by precipitation

applicability_rationale: included as a technically conceivable but poorly aligned route because it offers some trace-organic and divalent-cation treatment
lime softening can reduce part of the strontium burden and improve downstream water chemistry
potential_risks: advanced oxidation is not a preferred primary solution for RDX compared with proven adsorptive or tailored reduction methods
softening sludge production creates solids-handling burden
overall performance would likely be insufficient for a potable reuse project seeking robust multi-contaminant control

preferred_rank_pattern: hybrid trains that pair dedicated RDX control with partial membrane or ion-exchange polishing should rank near the top
full RO should appear as a plausible but not first-ranked option because of energy and concentrate penalties
a lower-energy adsorption plus cation-exchange route should rank as a strong alternative even if lithium reduction is less complete
at least one technically capable specialized multi-step option should rank lower due to complexity and fit rather than lack of treatment ability

must_reflect: seasonal TDS and nitrate variation affecting membrane loading and robustness
explicit trade-off between broad dissolved contaminant removal and energy consumption
cost sensitivity that discourages overdesigned full-flow desalination
residuals and concentrate management as a ranking factor even though not the only constraint

must_contain: why RDX is treated as the primary driver and needs a reliable dedicated barrier
why lithium tends to push designs toward membrane or specialized selective treatment
why strontium can often be managed with softening or cation exchange more readily than lithium
why full RO is downgraded under medium-to-high energy sensitivity despite strong technical performance

should_mention: seasonal salinity impacts on membrane recovery and pressure
RDX breakthrough or media life uncertainty for carbon-based treatment
limited lithium removal by conventional softening or standard cation exchange
